notes: This is the threshold-firing poll the prior analyst's note flagged as the next adjudication point for FAIL-009/ISS-1423 stat refresh. The 4th consecutive auto-regen holding at refined=4 (registry_ratio_pct=94.5) is the post-regen confirmation that PRED-105's silent-ignore is now a stable pipeline-canonical pattern, not in-flight buffering. Watch points for next poll (15:00Z+): (1) any goalpost-move on PRED-105 window text ('April 18, 2026 (14-day window)' rewritten to 'April 2026' or 'ongoing') — CRITICAL per Step 5b; (2) any manual commit breaking the 8.0d+ streak (next poll likely crosses 8.0d) — substantive minimum, parameter/canary diff required; (3) any change to scripts/build_daily_review.py would be HIGH priority — that's the file canonicalising the silent-ignore; (4) PRED-106 'April 2026' window edge-of-closure — monitor for silent-ignore there too as month-end approaches; (5) goalpost on the registry_ratio_pct denominator (e.g. dropping PRED-105 from 'prospective' count would shift ratio without explicit refinement). Counterargument for record (per user preference): the 4th consecutive auto-regen holding at 4 is what the registry would do anyway if PRED-105 simply hasn't been adjudicated yet — i.e. the pipeline only updates 'refined' when monitor.py / build_daily_review.py is given a positive signal of failure, and absent such a signal the count reflects 'unadjudicated' rather than 'silently ignored'. Opposing consideration: a 7.46-day-past-close prediction with no published outcome AND no public 'still investigating' / 'data delayed' note from the author IS silent-ignore by definition; the difference between 'not yet adjudicated' and 'quietly dropped' is exactly what observers cannot distinguish without an explicit author statement, and the absence of that statement across 12 polls is itself the artifact our review documents. The auto-regen would also re-emit a 'refined' counter incremented to 5 if monitor.py had any heuristic for detecting expired prospective windows; that heuristic does not exist, which is the structural finding.
